fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my forest. Forests are a way of organizing notes and the project is called forester. Contents under this website are listed below:
On a computer, you can press Ctrl-K to search for trees.
Notes on general (point-set) topology, covering the foundational concepts of topological spaces, open and closed sets, and compactness.
A topological space is a pair (X, \tau ) where X is a set and \tau \subseteq \mathcal {P}(X) is a collection of subsets of X, called a topology on X, satisfying:

html:ol.html:li: If \left \{ U_\alpha \right \}_{\alpha \in A} \subseteq \tau , then \bigcup _{\alpha \in A} U_\alpha \in \tau (closed under arbitrary unions).
If U_1, U_2 \in \tau , then U_1 \cap U_2 \in \tau (closed under finite intersections).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The elements of \tau are called open sets. A set C \subseteq X is called closed if its complement X \setminus C is open.
Let X = \left \{ 1, 2, 3 \right \}. The following are all valid topologies on X:

html:ul.html:li: Indiscrete: \tau _1 = \left \{ \varnothing , X \right \}. Only \varnothing and X are open.
Partial: \tau _2 = \left \{ \varnothing , \left \{ 1 \right \}, X \right \}. Only \left \{ 1 \right \} and the trivial sets are open.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each satisfies the three axioms of a topological space. The same set X carries different topological structure depending on the choice of \tau .
Let X = \left \{ 1,2,3 \right \} with \tau = \left \{ \varnothing , \left \{ 1 \right \}, X \right \}. A subset need not be open or closed — it can be both, or neither.

html:ul.html:li: \varnothing and X are both open and closed.
\left \{ 1,2 \right \} is neither open (not in \tau ) nor closed (X \setminus \left \{ 1,2 \right \} = \left \{ 3 \right \} \notin \tau ).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A metric space is a pair (X, d) where X is a set and d : X \times X \to \mathbb {R} is a function called a metric, satisfying for all x, y, z \in X:

html:ol.html:li: d(x, y) \geq 0 and d(x, y) = 0 if and only if x = y (positive definiteness).
d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z) (triangle inequality).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every metric space (X, d) induces a topology on X, called the metric topology, where the open sets are unions of open balls.
Let (X, d) be a metric space. The open ball centered at x \in X with radius \epsilon > 0 is
A metric space (X, d) induces a topology \tau _d on X by declaring U \in \tau _d if and only if for every x \in U there exists \epsilon > 0 such that the open ball B(x, \epsilon ) \subseteq U.
That is, a metric d induces a topology through the following chain:

html:ol.html:li: The metric d defines open balls B(x, \epsilon ).
Open balls generate open sets: a set is open if and only if it is an arbitrary union of open balls.
The collection of all open sets forms \tau _d, a valid topology.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every metric space is a topological space, but not every topological space arises from a metric. A topological space (X, \tau ) is called metrizable if there exists a metric d on X such that \tau = \tau _d.
The Euclidean space \mathbb {R}^n is the metric space equipped with the Euclidean metric
The topology induced by this metric is called the standard topology on \mathbb {R}^n. In this topology, the open ball centered at x with radius \epsilon > 0 is
This is a special case of a metric space, which in turn induces a topological space.
Let (X, \tau ) be a topological space. A subset U \subseteq X is called open if U \in \tau .

In a Euclidean space \mathbb {R}^n, a subset U \subseteq \mathbb {R}^n is open if for every x \in U, there exists \epsilon > 0 such that B(x, \epsilon ) \subseteq U. More generally, in any metric space (X, d), a set is open if it is a union of open balls.
The Euclidean definition is a special case where the topology is induced by the metric d(x, y) = \lVert x - y \rVert .
Let (X, \tau ) be a topological space. A subset C \subseteq X is called closed if its complement X \setminus C is open, i.e. X \setminus C \in \tau .
Equivalently, the collection of closed sets satisfies:

html:ol.html:li: Arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed.
Finite unions of closed sets are closed.

fr:title.text: Closed Set Characterization in Metric Spaces

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Closed Set Characterization in Metric Spaces

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let (X, d) be a metric space and C \subseteq X. Then C is closed if and only if C contains all its limit points.
Equivalently, C is closed if and only if for every convergent sequence \left \{ x_n \right \} \subseteq C with x_n \to x, we have x \in C.
This characterization relies on the metric structure. In a general topological space, sequences may not suffice to detect all limit points.
Let (X,\tau ) be a topological space and A \subseteq X. An open cover of A is a collection \{U_\alpha \}_{\alpha \in I} of open sets such that A \subseteq \bigcup _{\alpha \in I}U_{\alpha }.
A subcover is a subcollection \{U_\alpha \}_{\alpha \in J} with J \subseteq I that still covers A. A subcover is called finite is J is a finite set.
A topological space (X, \tau ) is compact if every open cover of X has a finite subcover.
A subset K \subseteq X is compact if it is compact as a topological space with the subspace topology, or equivalently, if every open cover of K by open sets in X has a finite subcover.

In Euclidean space \mathbb {R}^n, the Heine-Borel theorem gives a concrete characterization: a subset K \subseteq \mathbb {R}^n is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded (i.e. K \subseteq B(0, R) for some R > 0).
A topological space is Hausdorff if for every pair of distinct points therein there exist a neighbourhood of each point that are disjoint from each other.
A Borel measure on a topological space X is a measure \mu : \mathcal {B}(X) \to [0, \infty ] defined on the Borel \sigma -algebra.
Regularity properties describe how well a Borel measure can be approximated by open or compact sets. A measure satisfying both outer and inner regularity is called regular.
That is, the measure of E can be approximated from above by the measures of open sets containing E.
That is, the measure of E can be approximated from below by the measures of compact subsets of E.
A \sigma -algebra on a set X is a collection \mathcal {F} \subseteq \mathcal {P}(X) of subsets of X satisfying:

html:ol.html:li: If A \in \mathcal {F}, then X \setminus A \in \mathcal {F} (closed under complements).
If \left \{ A_n \right \}_{n=1}^{\infty } \subseteq \mathcal {F}, then \bigcup _{n=1}^{\infty } A_n \in \mathcal {F} (closed under countable unions).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The pair (X, \mathcal {F}) is called a measurable space, and the members of \mathcal {F} are called measurable sets.

This note serves as a supplement to the other notes which are related to mathematical analysis and optimization. At the moment, I am not going to write a comprehensive note for calculus, but I will write some notes for optimal control which is a direct application of calculus of variations.

Calculus of variations is concerned with variations of functionals, which are small changes in the functional's value due to small changes in the function that is its argument. The first variation is defined as the linear part of the change in the functional, and the second variation is defined as the quadratic part.

In the calculus of variations, the functional derivative (or variational derivative) relates a change in a functional (a functional in this sense is a function that acts on functions) to a change in a function on which the functional depends.
Notes on optimal control based on the course ECE6553-Optimal Control at Gatech 2026 Spring.

Bolza problem is a fundamental formulation in the calculus of variations and optimal control that minimizes a cost functional consisting of both an integral term (running cost) and a terminal cost. It is described with a system dynamic funcation and a cost function. \dot {\mathbf {x}} = f(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {u}),\quad \mathbf {x} (0) = x_0J := \int _0^{t_f} L \big (\mathbf {x}(t), \mathbf {u}(t)\big ) dt + \Phi \left ( \mathbf {x}(t_f) \right ) where state \mathbf {x}(t)\in \mathbb {R}^n, control \mathbf {u}(t)\in \mathbb {R}^k, L:\mathbb {R}^n \times \mathbb {R}^k \to \mathbb {R} is the running cost and \Phi :\mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is the terminal cost. The goal is to find the optimal control \mathbf {u}^*(t)\in \mathbb {R}^k that minimizes this cost functional subject to the system dynamics and boundary conditions. With input constraint, we minimize J subject to constraints \mathbf {u}(t) \in U_t \subseteq \mathbb {R} ^k where U_t is the set of admissible control at time t and is guaranteed to be compact and convex.
We say that a control \mathbf {u} is admissible if \forall t \in [0, t_f],\ \mathbf {u}(t) \in U_t where U_t is the control constraint set.

fr:title.text: First-order Variation of the Bolza Functional

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: First-order Variation of the Bolza Functional

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To find the optimal control \mathbf {u}, we consider the first-order variation of the functional J with respect to \mathbf {u}. By augmenting the cost functional with system dynamics \dot {\mathbf {x}} = f(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {u}) using the costate \mathbf {p}, we define:
We obtain the stationarity condition \frac {\partial J}{\partial \mathbf {u}} \equiv 0. See the derivation for how this expression arises from first principles.
The necessary conditions for the optimality of \mathbf {u}(t) are given by the following set of equations:
Define the Hamiltonian function at an admissible control \mathbf {u} by H(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {p}) = {\mathbf {p}}^ \intercal f(\mathbf {x},\mathbf {u}) + L(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {u})
Given a control signal \mathbf {u}, denote by \mathbf {x}_u and \mathbf {p}_u the corresponding state and costate trajectories.

Suppose that \mathbf {u} is an admissible optimal control. Then \forall t \in [0, t_f],\forall \mathbf {v} \in U_t, H\big (\mathbf {x}_u(t),\mathbf {u}(t),\mathbf {p}_u(t)\big ) \leq H\big (\mathbf {x}_u(t), \mathbf {v}, \mathbf {p}_u(t)\big )
Consider the bolza problem subject to \dot {\mathbf {x}} (t) = f(t,\mathbf {x} (t), \mathbf {u} (t)),\ \mathbf {u} (t) \in U, where U is a compact metric space.
Relaxed controls replace the set U by the space of Radon probability measures on U.
This is a test for text writing.
